skill.name: routeros-container
skill.description: Guides RouterOS users through enabling, configuring, and troubleshooting the MikroTik /container subsystem for OCI containers.
skill.summary: Configure and manage MikroTik RouterOS containers with version-aware networking, storage, and REST guidance.
skill.author: tikoci

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, network, filesystem, and weak-crypto patterns, but review shows these are Markdown documentation examples rather than executable skill code. The skill is not malicious, but it provides instructions for real RouterOS changes, container networking, NAT exposure, registry pulls, and REST API access, so it should publish with an operational security warning.

medium_findings.title: Operational Router Configuration Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill provides RouterOS commands that enable container support, install packages, create VETH interfaces, configure NAT, start containers, and add apps. These are legitimate administrative examples, but applying them to production routers can expose services or change device posture if users do not review values first.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file clearly contains router administration examples, and several examples change package state, firewall NAT, containers, and apps. The concern is operational misuse rather than malicious code execution.

medium_findings.title: Insecure Credential Transport Example
medium_findings.description: The REST API example uses an HTTP base URL with Basic authentication and an admin credential placeholder. This can normalize unsafe management access if copied without changing to HTTPS, stronger accounts, and scoped credentials.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The example explicitly combines http://192.168.1.1/rest with a Basic Authorization header. It is sample documentation, but the insecure transport pattern is concrete.

low_findings.title: Static Command Findings Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The many external command detections occur inside Markdown code blocks or inline RouterOS path names. They are not scripts that the skill executes automatically, so command injection or local shell execution was not confirmed.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review shows Markdown documentation and fenced examples rather than executable files. No automation wrapper or runtime command executor was found in the scanned file.

low_findings.title: Network Findings Are Router Management Examples
low_findings.description: Fetch calls, hardcoded URLs, and private IP addresses are used to demonstrate RouterOS REST and registry configuration. They do not show data exfiltration or hidden third-party communication by the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network patterns are visible in explanatory TypeScript or RouterOS examples. No evidence shows automatic network access by the skill outside user-requested guidance.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Path Traversal Finding Is a Relative Documentation Link
low_findings.description: The path traversal alert points to a Markdown reference to a related skill document. It is a relative link for documentation navigation, not file access logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged sequence appears only in a Markdown link to another reference file. There is no file read, write, upload, or path normalization code at that location.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: Review did not find override instructions, claims of pre-approval, instructions to skip analysis, or text posing as higher-priority system messages.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected skill text contains normal metadata and RouterOS documentation. Keyword search and manual review found no prompt-injection language.

dangerous_patterns.title: HTTP Basic Authentication in REST Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The REST section demonstrates Basic authentication over an HTTP router management endpoint. Users should adapt this to HTTPS, restricted accounts, and local trusted networks before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The insecure management pattern is explicit in the example. It is documentation, so risk depends on whether users copy it directly.

dangerous_patterns.title: Port Forwarding and Masquerade Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The firewall section includes NAT masquerade and destination NAT examples. These are common RouterOS tasks but can expose container services if copied without scoping rules.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are concrete and affect network exposure. They are legitimate examples, but their security depends on production firewall context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Container Image Pull From Public Registry
dangerous_patterns.description: The registry pull example configures Docker Hub and pulls an Alpine image. This is expected functionality, but users should pin trusted images and verify supply chain controls.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The public registry configuration is visible and intentional. It is not malicious, but it introduces normal container supply chain risk.

content.value_statement: RouterOS containers require version-specific commands, storage planning, and careful network setup. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical guidance for MikroTik /container deployments.
actual_capabilities: Explains RouterOS container prerequisites, device-mode requirements, and supported CPU architectures.
Provides VETH, bridge, NAT, and Layer 2 networking examples for container deployments.
Documents environment variable, mount, and property name differences across RouterOS versions.
Guides local tar imports, registry pulls, and RouterOS-specific OCI image format constraints.
Shows CLI and REST API patterns for container lifecycle operations and status checks.
Compares manual container setup with the RouterOS /app workflow for common deployment choices.
limitations: It is documentation and does not connect to routers or validate live configuration automatically.
Examples use placeholder addresses, passwords, image names, and storage paths that users must replace.
RouterOS behavior can change by version, package state, hardware model, and device-mode settings.
It does not provide a full hardening checklist for exposed container services.

use_cases.title: Deploy a MikroTik Container Service
use_cases.description: Plan package installation, storage, VETH networking, and lifecycle commands for a single RouterOS container.

use_cases.title: Automate Container Management
use_cases.description: Use RouterOS REST API examples to list, start, stop, poll, and delete containers safely.

use_cases.title: Troubleshoot Version Differences
use_cases.description: Check property names, env list behavior, mount support, and image import rules across RouterOS releases.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Basic Container
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me deploy one RouterOS container on MikroTik hardware. Ask for my RouterOS version, architecture, storage, and network requirements before giving commands.

prompt_templates.title: Design Container Networking
prompt_templates.prompt: Design RouterOS VETH, bridge, IP address, NAT, and port forwarding settings for a container that serves one TCP application on my LAN.

prompt_templates.title: Review a RouterOS Container Config
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this RouterOS /container plan for version compatibility, storage safety, firewall exposure, and missing lifecycle steps.

prompt_templates.title: Build REST Automation Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a safe RouterOS REST workflow for starting, stopping, polling, and deleting containers. Include retry behavior and security assumptions.

output_examples.input: I want to run Pi-hole on RouterOS 7.22 with external USB storage.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step deployment plan covering package checks, device-mode confirmation, external disk paths, VETH and bridge setup, container creation, logging, and safe firewall review.

output_examples.input: My script deletes a container before it fully stops.
output_examples.output: A REST troubleshooting explanation that recommends polling the running status until it reports false before sending the delete request.

output_examples.input: Should I use manual /container commands or /app YAML?
output_examples.output: A comparison of manual networking control, Layer 2 access, multi-container convenience, and port-forwarded application deployment tradeoffs.

best_practices: Confirm RouterOS version, architecture, package state, and device-mode before applying container commands.
Use external storage for container roots and volumes to reduce wear on internal flash memory.
Review NAT, port forwarding, registry, and REST examples for security before using them on production routers.
anti_patterns: Do not copy placeholder passwords, IP addresses, or image names into a production router unchanged.
Do not expose container services with broad firewall rules before defining trusted sources and ports.
Do not assume Docker behavior matches RouterOS container behavior for local image imports or networking.

faq.question: Does this skill run commands on my MikroTik router?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. You choose whether to run any command on your router.

faq.question: Which RouterOS versions does it discuss?
faq.answer: It focuses on RouterOS 7.x and includes notes for 7.18, 7.20, 7.21, and 7.22 behavior.

faq.question: Can it help with VETH and bridge networking?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains VETH interfaces, container bridges, gateway addresses, NAT, and Layer 2 bridge cases.

faq.question: Does it cover REST API automation?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples for listing, starting, stopping, polling, and deleting RouterOS containers.

faq.question: Can it build container images for RouterOS?
faq.answer: It explains RouterOS image format requirements and architecture mapping, but it does not build images by itself.

faq.question: What should I review before using the examples?
faq.answer: Review device-mode changes, firewall exposure, credentials, storage paths, image trust, and RouterOS version compatibility.
